text: Mr. President. Finally.
I've been trying to get on your calendar for three weeks. Three weeks.
Do you know how much can change in three weeks? Historically speaking?
A lot. The answer is a lot.
Look -- I'll be straight with you, because I think you're a straight-talk kind of person.
I run the largest private defense contractor in the Western hemisphere.
Thirty-two thousand employees. Seventeen active government contracts. Twelve patents pending.
And right now, I am looking at the geopolitical situation unfolding on my screens,
and I am seeing something most people are too afraid to say out loud.
An opportunity.
Not just for my company, Mr. President. But for America.
I'd like five minutes of your time. Just five. Can I get five minutes?
Perfect!
So. Here's the thing about peace, Mr. President.
Peace is -- and I say this with the utmost respect for the concept -- stagnation.
You look at every major leap in American technology, infrastructure, economic dominance
every single one of them has roots in a conflict.
The computer? Defense project. GPS? Military. Half the medicine in your cabinet?
Conflict-accelerated research. I'm not making this up. This is documented history.
War is, at its core, the greatest innovation engine ever invented.
And right now, that engine is sitting on the launchpad.
All it needs is someone with the vision -- and the authority -- to press go.
That's you, Mr. President. That's only ever been you.
Alright! Straight to it. I respect that. Genuinely.
Bottom line: I believe this country is on the edge of something historic.
The question is whether we step forward deliberately, or get pushed.
I'd rather we step. Wouldn't you?
Dangerous. Yes. That's actually a great word for it.
Do you know what else is dangerous? Falling behind.
Russia isn't sitting on its hands right now, Mr. President.
They are building. Testing. Pushing boundaries that have been sitting still for thirty years.
The dangerous argument isn't the one I'm making.
The dangerous argument is the one that says: 'wait and see'.
Because by the time you see -- it may be too late to do anything about it.
I've run the projections. My analysts have run the projections.
The window for a favorable engagement is not open indefinitely.
It is open right now.

links.label: I won't be rushed into a war

text: Happy to.
Let's talk about what a decisive move looks like -- economically.
First eighteen months of active engagement: defense procurement goes up four hundred percent.
American manufacturing gets a jolt it hasn't seen since the forties.
Unemployment drops. Innovation spikes. Allies start picking sides -- and they pick us.
Because they always pick the side that looks like it's going to win.
And we would win, Mr. President. I have no doubt about that.
My hardware is in forty-three countries. I know exactly what the other side is working with.
We outclass them. Comprehensively.
The only question is whether we use that advantage -- or let it depreciate.
Not interested in war. I hear that a lot, actually.
You know who else wasn't interested in war?
Every president who ended up in one.
The thing about conflict, sir, is that your interest in it is largely irrelevant.
What matters is whether you're ready for it.
And right now -- are you ready? Is this country ready?
Because I can make it ready. That's not a sales pitch. That's a capability statement.
Let me show you what I mean.
We move first, we move fast, and we control the narrative.
Right now, their supply chains are stretched. Their eastern flank is undermanned.
Three of their satellite states are quietly, very quietly, looking for an exit.
My intelligence -- and yes, I have intelligence, I invest heavily in intelligence --
suggests that six months from now, that picture looks very different.
They shore up the flank. The satellite states fall back in line. The window closes.
Six months, Mr. President. That's the delta between a clean operation and a prolonged one.
I know which one I'd rather fund. And I think you know which one you'd rather run.
Nobody is rushing you, Mr. President.
I want to be very clear about that.
I am providing information. Context. A perspective that isn't filtered through
seventeen layers of bureaucracy and two congressional subcommittees.
You asked for straight talk. This is straight talk.
The timeline isn't mine. It's theirs.
I'm just the one with enough clearance -- unofficial clearance, but clearance nonetheless --
to tell you what that timeline actually looks like.
What you do with that is entirely your call.
Of course I have an agenda. I'm a busy man.
However, I think transparency is important, so let me be completely transparent.
My company profits in a wartime economy. Significantly. I won't pretend otherwise.
But here's what I'd push back on --
Every person in that room advising you has an agenda.
The difference is I'm the only one honest enough to tell you what mine is.
And my agenda happens to align perfectly with American strategic dominance.
So yes. I have an agenda. And it is, incidentally, very good for this country.
I don't see the conflict there. Do you?
Simple. Three things.
One: you green-light an accelerated procurement cycle. My manufacturing is already staged.
We can have materiel in position within sixty days. Sixty.
Two: you authorize an expanded intelligence-sharing framework.
My satellite network covers gaps that yours currently does not. I've seen your coverage maps.
Three -- and this is the one that matters most --
you stop treating this conflict as something to be avoided
and start treating it as something to be won.
That mental shift, Mr. President -- that is worth more than any weapons system I can sell you.
It's the difference between a president who managed a crisis and one who ended it.
Which one do you want to be?
You know what? I respect that. A leader of your caliber needs lines to not cross.
I really do respect that, in a way.
I'll leave my contact details with your secretary.
Because I think -- and I say this with genuine admiration for your conviction --
I think the situation is going to change your mind before I have to.
And when it does, you'll want someone on the line who actually knows what to do.
Just one handshake deal, and world is all yours
My line is always open, Mr. President.
Always.
First strike is such a loaded term.
Politically loaded, I mean. Strategically it's just... sequencing.
Who acts first. Who sets the conditions.
Every winning operation in military history began with someone deciding
that waiting for the other side to move was the less intelligent option.
I'm not telling you to start a war, Mr. President.
I'm telling you that if a war is coming -- and I believe it is --
the side that starts it on their terms wins it on their terms.
That's not ideology. That's physics.
Diplomacy.
Okay. I can work with that framing, actually.
Because here's the thing about diplomacy -- it only works from a position of strength.
You go to that table with full procurement, full coverage, full readiness --
and suddenly Romanov is a lot more interested in talking.
You go to that table the way you are right now?
She'll smile, she'll shake your hand, and she'll go home and do exactly what she was going to do.
I'm not against diplomacy, Mr. President.
I'm against weak diplomacy.
Let me make yours strong.
Now we're talking.
So. My current production pipeline has three platforms ready for fast-track delivery.
Aerial suppression systems -- next generation, nothing like what's in the field right now.
Rapid-response ground units. Modular. Deployable in seventy-two hours.
And the one I'm most excited about -- I won't get into details on an open line --
but let's just say it makes the other side's early warning systems significantly less reliable.
Total package, sixty-day deployment, full logistics support.
I'm prepared to offer the government a preferred rate.
Out of patriotism.
And because the volume is frankly extraordinary.
Do we have a deal, Mr. President?
I'm not trying to push you to do anything reckless. Truly.
But I would ask you to consider one thing before you close the door completely.
Authorization isn't the same as initiation.
All I'm asking for tonight is readiness.
Have the capability. Have the option. Keep the door cracked.
You don't have to walk through it.
But right now? That door is locked from the outside.
Let me give you the key.
That's all.
Most Excellent.
Mr. President, you have just made an exceptionally intelligent decision.
I don't say that lightly. I've met a lot of presidents. Well -- two. But still.
My team will have the framework documents to your procurement office by morning.
Classified, obviously. Eyes-only. Very clean paper trail.
Well -- not too clean. You want it to look like it went through the normal process.
We're good at that.
It has been a genuine pleasure, sir.
America is going to look very different in six months.
Very different.
In a good way.
Probably.
Need a moment? Take all the time you need.
I do want to mention -- and I'm not saying this to pressure you --
three of my other government contacts have reached out this week.
Other governments. Very interested in this same package.
You know, the powerful kind. The kind that openly opposes our great nation.
I've held them off because I believe in America first. I genuinely do.
But I can only hold them off for so long before I have a fiduciary obligation to respond.
You understand.
No pressure.
Truly.
I'll give you that moment to think, now.
Mr. President. How nice of you to return my call.
I've been trying to get on your calendar for three weeks. Three weeks.
Not just because I myself am a busy man, but it seems that you are too.
Look -- I'll be straight with you, I was watching that interview you did with Wilkins earlier,
and I must say, I believe that I can change your mind on your stance on some topics.
You see, I run the largest private defense contractor this side of the world.
Thirty-two thousand employees. Seventeen active government contracts. Twelve patents pending.
And right now, I am looking at the geopolitical situation unfolding on my screens,
and I am seeing something most people are too afraid to say out loud.
An opportunity.
Not just for my company, Mr. President. But for America.
I'd like five minutes of your time. Just five. Can I get five minutes?
Perfect!
So. Here's the thing about peace, Mr. President.
Unlike what Wilkins and the rest of those plebians believe, peace is never the answer.
Look at every major leap in American technology, infrastructure, economic dominance
every single one of them exists because of conflict.
Peace will never invent the computer, the GPS, nor whatever's in you medicine cabinet.
Conflict-accelerated research. I'm not making this up. This is documented history.
War is, at its core, the greatest innovation engine ever invented.
And right now, that engine is sitting on the launchpad.
All it needs is someone with the vision -- and the authority -- to press go.
That's you, Mr. President. That's only ever been you.
Mr. President. How nice of you to return my call.
I've been trying to get on your calendar for three weeks. Three weeks.
Not just because I myself am a busy man, but it seems that you are too.
Look -- I'll be straight with you, I was watching that interview you did with Wilkins earlier,
and I must say, I believe that you and I are cut from the same cloth.
You see, I admire you and your reputation.
The way you handled that interview? Perfection
And now, in the midst of a major geopolitical situation,
I see an oppurtunity for both of us.
I'd like five minutes of your time. Just five. Can I get five minutes?
